## Code Review

This pull request introduces support for Tailscale MagicDNS resolution and adds a warning mechanism when connecting to tunnel-bound hosts without an active VPN. The implementation includes a new `TailscaleResolver` for unicast DNS queries, a `TailscaleProxyData` class for SSH connections, and UI updates to inform users about resolution status and potential VPN issues. My review identified a critical bug in the DNS resolution fallback logic where a timeout prematurely aborts the entire process, as well as two instances where unsafe access to the `manager` object could lead to null pointer exceptions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The [consumer version of Gemini Code Assist on GitHub](https://developers.google.com/gemini-code-assist/docs/review-repo-code) is being sunset. Starting **June 18, 2026**, new organization installations will be blocked, and all code review activity will officially cease on **July 17, 2026**.
> For more details on the timeline and next steps, please review the [Help Documentation](https://developers.google.com/gemini-code-assist/docs/deprecations/consumer-code-review).

![critical](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/critical.svg)

### Critical Bug: Premature abort in DNS resolution fallback

When `deadlineNanos` is `null` (the default, meaning no global timeout was specified), `MdnsResolver.receiveTimeoutMillis(null, receiveDeadline)` is called. 

Inside `receiveTimeoutMillis`, the only non-null deadline is `receiveDeadline`. Once `System.nanoTime() >= receiveDeadline` (i.e., the 1-second receive window for this specific query attempt expires), `receiveTimeoutMillis` returns `null`.

Because of the `?: return null` Elvis operator, `queryViaUnicast` immediately returns `null` and exits the entire function. This completely aborts the resolution process, meaning we **never** try the next query attempt, the next query type (e.g., `TYPE_AAAA`), or the next DNS server!

To fix this, we should only return `null` if the global `deadlineNanos` has expired. If only the local `receiveDeadline` has expired, we should `break` the loop to proceed to the next attempt/type/server.

```kotlin
                                    if (deadlineNanos != null && System.nanoTime() >= deadlineNanos) {
                                        return null
                                    }
                                    val remainingNanos = receiveDeadline - System.nanoTime()
                                    if (remainingNanos <= 0) {
                                        break
                                    }
                                    socket.soTimeout = (remainingNanos / NANOS_PER_MILLI).toInt().coerceAtLeast(1)
                                    socket.receive(packet)
```

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

### Defensive Programming: Safe access to `manager`

If `manager` is null, `manager?.res?.getString(...)` will return `null`. Passing `null` to `bridge?.outputLine` can lead to a `NullPointerException` or parameter mismatch crash if the method expects a non-nullable `String`.

Since we cannot check the VPN state or load the warning string without a valid `manager` instance, we should safely bind `manager` and return early if it is null.

```suggestion
    internal fun warnIfVpnInactive(host: Host) {
        val mgr = manager ?: return
        val name = host.hostname
        if (!TailscaleResolver.isTailscaleHostname(name) && !TailscaleResolver.isTailscaleAddress(name)) {
            return
        }
        if (mgr.isVpnActive()) return
        bridge?.outputLine(mgr.res.getString(R.string.terminal_vpn_inactive, name))
    }
```

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

### Defensive Programming: Safe access to `manager` in `tailscaleProxyData`

Similarly to `warnIfVpnInactive`, if `manager` is null, `manager?.res?.getString(...)` will return `null`. We should safely bind `manager` using `let` to avoid passing `null` to `bridge?.outputLine`.

```kotlin
    private fun tailscaleProxyData(host: Host): TailscaleProxyData = TailscaleProxyData(TailscaleResolver(), host.ipVersion) { address ->
        manager?.let { mgr ->
            bridge?.outputLine(
                mgr.res.getString(R.string.terminal_tailscale_resolved, host.hostname, address.hostAddress),
            )
        }
    }
```

![critical](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/critical.svg)

### Critical Bug: Premature abort in DNS resolution fallback

When `deadlineNanos` is `null` (the default, meaning no global timeout was specified), `MdnsResolver.receiveTimeoutMillis(null, receiveDeadline)` is called. 

Inside `receiveTimeoutMillis`, the only non-null deadline is `receiveDeadline`. Once `System.nanoTime() >= receiveDeadline` (i.e., the 1-second receive window for this specific query attempt expires), `receiveTimeoutMillis` returns `null`.

Because of the `?: return null` Elvis operator, `queryViaUnicast` immediately returns `null` and exits the entire function. This completely aborts the resolution process, meaning we **never** try the next query attempt, the next query type (e.g., `TYPE_AAAA`), or the next DNS server!

To fix this, we should only return `null` if the global `deadlineNanos` has expired. If only the local `receiveDeadline` has expired, we should `break` the loop to proceed to the next attempt/type/server.

```kotlin
                                    if (deadlineNanos != null && System.nanoTime() >= deadlineNanos) {
                                        return null
                                    }
                                    val remainingNanos = receiveDeadline - System.nanoTime()
                                    if (remainingNanos <= 0) {
                                        break
                                    }
                                    socket.soTimeout = (remainingNanos / NANOS_PER_MILLI).toInt().coerceAtLeast(1)
                                    socket.receive(packet)
```

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

### Defensive Programming: Safe access to `manager`

If `manager` is null, `manager?.res?.getString(...)` will return `null`. Passing `null` to `bridge?.outputLine` can lead to a `NullPointerException` or parameter mismatch crash if the method expects a non-nullable `String`.

Since we cannot check the VPN state or load the warning string without a valid `manager` instance, we should safely bind `manager` and return early if it is null.

```suggestion
    internal fun warnIfVpnInactive(host: Host) {
        val mgr = manager ?: return
        val name = host.hostname
        if (!TailscaleResolver.isTailscaleHostname(name) && !TailscaleResolver.isTailscaleAddress(name)) {
            return
        }
        if (mgr.isVpnActive()) return
        bridge?.outputLine(mgr.res.getString(R.string.terminal_vpn_inactive, name))
    }
```

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

### Defensive Programming: Safe access to `manager` in `tailscaleProxyData`

Similarly to `warnIfVpnInactive`, if `manager` is null, `manager?.res?.getString(...)` will return `null`. We should safely bind `manager` using `let` to avoid passing `null` to `bridge?.outputLine`.

```kotlin
    private fun tailscaleProxyData(host: Host): TailscaleProxyData = TailscaleProxyData(TailscaleResolver(), host.ipVersion) { address ->
        manager?.let { mgr ->
            bridge?.outputLine(
                mgr.res.getString(R.string.terminal_tailscale_resolved, host.hostname, address.hostAddress),
            )
        }
    }
```
